mnemonics.title: AGILE Knowledge Transfer Methods
mnemonics.content: PARE: Pair Programming (XP), Agile Retrospectives, Daily Stand-ups, Information Radiators/Embodiedly Accessible (Wiki, Boards). These four practices form the core of knowledge sharing in agile.

mnemonics.title: Types of Knowledge in Agile
mnemonics.content: E.T.C.: Explicit (documented), Tacit (experiential), Collaborative (co-created through interaction). Remember: Agile excels at converting Tacit to Explicit through collaboration.

mnemonics.title: Bus Factor Mitigation
mnemonics.content: C.P.R.: Collective ownership, Pair programming, Retrospectives (capture lessons). These three practices ensure knowledge isn't concentrated in few individuals.

acronyms.usage: An agile methodology emphasizing pair programming, continuous feedback, and technical excellence for high-quality knowledge-intensive software development.
A shared team agreement establishing criteria for considering work complete; ensures consistent knowledge standards across all sprint deliverables.
A collaborative approach to requirements where examples (tests) define expected behavior; converts domain knowledge into executable specifications.
In Kanban, limiting WIP helps focus on completing work rather than starting new tasks, enabling better knowledge consolidation and faster feedback.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a circular diagram: Planning (acquires knowledge) → Development (applies knowledge) → Review (validates knowledge) → Retrospective (reflects on knowledge). Each iteration reinforces and expands the team's collective knowledge.

An iceberg model: Above water (10%) = Explicit (documents, specs), Below water (90%) = Tacit (intuition, experience). Agile practices like pair programming dive below the surface to transfer tacit knowledge.

A simple stick figure diagram showing team members with connecting lines. Fewer connections = higher risk. Agile aims for a fully connected mesh where every member knows every other member's expertise.

analogies.analogy: Like building a puzzle: In waterfall, you try to see the complete picture upfront. In agile, you build pieces iteratively and let the picture emerge. Each sprint adds pieces, and feedback corrects placement—knowledge accumulates organically.

Like a master-apprentice relationship in traditional crafts. The master (navigator) guides, corrects, and shares tricks of the trade while the apprentice (driver) practices and learns by doing. Both contribute to the final work.
Like a ship's captain's log. Each voyage (sprint) records what worked, what didn't, and navigation lessons. Future captains (teams) consult this log to avoid dangers and replicate successes.
